factors._comment: Shared factor dictionary. Every profile (environment or species) references these metric ids. 'evidence' labels how well-supported the factor is: 'strong' (physical/biological mechanism, widely documented), 'moderate' (consistent angling literature + experience), 'low' (folklore/solunar — included transparently, weighted low). Note templates are used to build plain-language 'why' strings; {v} is the formatted value.

tide_speed.label: Tide flow
tide_speed.description: Rate of water movement, normalised 0-1 against the day's fastest flow. Moving water pushes bait and triggers feeding; slack water is generally slow fishing.

tide_mins_to_turn.label: Time to tide change
tide_mins_to_turn.description: Minutes until the next high or low. Many species bite in a window either side of the change.

tide_range.label: Tidal range
tide_range.description: High-minus-low for the day. Bigger (spring) tides move more water and more bait.
tide_range.note_pos: big spring tides moving plenty of water
tide_range.note_neg: small neap tides, little water movement

light_phase.label: Light
light_phase.description: Dawn / day / dusk / night, from computed sun times. Low-light changeovers are prime feeding windows for most species.

moon_illum.label: Moon phase
moon_illum.description: Fraction of moon illuminated. Solunar theory is weakly evidenced; the real, physical effect of the moon (bigger tides at new/full) is already captured by tidal range, so this factor carries a deliberately small weight.

pressure.label: Barometric pressure
pressure.description: Sea-level pressure. Settled high pressure generally correlates with steadier bites; deep lows mean unstable weather.

pressure_trend.label: Pressure trend (24h)
pressure_trend.description: Change over the past 24 hours. Stable or gently falling pressure often fishes well; a sharp rise after a front is a classic shutdown.
pressure_trend.note_neg: pressure swinging hard after a change

wind_kn.label: Wind speed
wind_kn.description: 10 m wind speed in knots. Mostly a comfort/safety/castability factor; critical offshore.

wind_onshore_kn.label: Onshore wind
wind_onshore_kn.description: Wind component blowing onto the coast (positive = onshore, negative = offshore/westerly). A light-moderate onshore stirs the surf zone and pushes bait to the beach; a strong onshore blows it out and brings weed.

swell_m.label: Swell height
swell_m.description: Primary swell height. White water is food and cover on rocks/beaches — until it becomes dangerous or unfishable. Irrelevant inside the estuary.

swell_period_s.label: Swell period
swell_period_s.description: Seconds between sets. Long-period swell carries far more energy at the same height — a key hidden danger on rock platforms.

sst_c.label: Water temp
sst_c.description: Sea surface temperature. Species presence and activity are strongly temperature-driven (e.g. kingfish switch on above ~19 °C).

rain_72h.label: Rain (72h)
rain_72h.description: Rain over the last 3 days — a turbidity/runoff proxy. Moderate runoff colours the water, washes food in and fires up estuary predators (classic mulloway trigger). Too much shuts everything down and dirties the harbour.
rain_72h.note_pos: runoff has coloured the water nicely
rain_72h.note_neg: system blown out with dirty flood water

rain_today.label: Rain forecast today
rain_today.description: Forecast rain for the day itself — mostly a comfort factor, heavy rain during the session is miserable and dangerous on rocks.

month.label: Season
month.description: Month of year, mapped through each species' seasonal presence curve (Sydney-specific).

safety.message: Swell is beyond safe limits for rock platforms today. Stay off the stones — no fish is worth it. Lifejackets are mandatory on many declared NSW rock platforms.

factors.justification: The defining rock variable. Some wash (0.7–1.2 m) oxygenates the zone, dislodges food and gives fish cover — flat, glassy days fish poorly off the stones. Above ~1.5 m the platform becomes unfishable, then lethal. This factor doubles as the safety signal: a low rock score IS the warning.
Energy scales with period at the same height — a 1.5 m swell at 14 s surges much further up a platform than 1.5 m at 8 s. Long-period days are the classic 'looked fine, got washed in' trap.
A light offshore or neutral wind keeps the face fishable; a strong onshore adds wind chop on top of swell and makes ledges wet and slippery well beyond what the swell number suggests.
Moving water concentrates the washes and gets pelagics and drummer feeding along the edges.

Dawn and dusk are the reliable bite windows off the rocks (tailor, salmon, kings at first light). Night rock fishing is productive for some species but the safety margin shrinks, so it is not scored as a peak.
Bigger tides push more water through the washes. Springs generally beat neaps off the stones.
Settled highs mean stable seas and steadier fishing; deep lows usually arrive with the exact swell and wind that close the rocks anyway.
A slow fall ahead of a change often fires the bite; a sharp post-frontal rise is a well-known shutdown across species.
LOW EVIDENCE. Solunar folklore, kept small and transparent. The moon's real effect (bigger tides) is already counted in tidal range.
Heavy rain during the session makes platforms slippery and visibility poor — a comfort and safety discount, not a bite factor.

environments.name: Beach
environments.tagline: Gutters, banks and the surf zone

factors.justification: The inverse of the rock story and the reason one all-Sydney score is meaningless: the same 2 m swell that closes the rocks builds the white water and defined gutters that tailor and salmon hunt in. Dead flat surf fishes poorly; 1–2 m is the sweet spot; past ~2.5 m the banks wash out.
A moderate onshore stirs the surf zone, pushes bait to the beach and puts oxygen and colour in the water. A hard onshore means weed, unmanageable sweep and casting into a wall.
Dusk into dark is the classic beach session (tailor at last light, mulloway and gummies after dark). Bright midday sun over shallow sand is the worst window.
A making or running tide moves food through the gutters; the top of the tide also lets you fish gutters that are too shallow at low.
Bigger tides flush the gutters harder and give a stronger run-in session in the evening.
Mid-period swell breaks cleanly and shapes gutters; very long period dumps and sweeps.
Same logic as everywhere: gentle fall good, sharp post-front rise poor.
Settled weather generally means a fishable, predictable beach.
Comfort factor — an exposed beach in driving rain is a short session.
LOW EVIDENCE. Solunar folklore, small weight, disclosed.

environments.name: Estuary
environments.tagline: Rivers, bays and the brackish reaches

factors.justification: In sheltered water the tide IS the weather. Run-out especially drains bait off the flats and out of the mangroves past waiting predators. Slack water is the classic dead hour.

The estuary's signature factor. 10–40 mm over a few days colours the water, flushes prawns and baitfish down and switches on mulloway and bream. Flood-level rain (>80 mm) blows the system out with fresh, dirty water and shuts it down.
Low light beats bright sun over shallow, clear estuary water; after-dark is productive around lit structure.
Swell never reaches here, so wind is only about casting, boat control and finding a lee — the estuary is where you go when everything else is blown out, which is exactly why it gets its own profile.
Spring tides move more water through the system and make the run-out window stronger.
Bream and whiting anglers widely report better sessions on settled highs; mechanism debated, correlation consistent.
Sharp swings shut shallow-water fish down harder than deep-water fish.
Coastal SST is only a rough proxy for estuary temperature, but warm-water months lift overall activity.
The hour either side of the change concentrates the bite around structure even as flow slows.
LOW EVIDENCE. Solunar folklore, small weight, disclosed.

environments.name: Harbour & wharf
environments.tagline: Structure, pylons and deep protected water

factors.justification: Harbour fishing is structure fishing, and structure only works when current pushes bait past it. Markers, moorings and pylons all fire on the run.
First light around harbour structure is the premium window (kingfish especially). Lit wharves keep producing after dark.
Protected water, so wind is about comfort and casting — and there is nearly always a lee shore somewhere in Sydney Harbour, which keeps this environment's floor high.
The harbour's headline species (kingfish) is strongly temperature-gated: bites switch on reliably once water passes ~19 °C.
The change is a classic kingfish and trevally trigger around markers and deep holes.
A little runoff is fine; big rain sends a dirty stormwater plume through the harbour and pushes clean-water species out.
More range, more current past the structure.
Settled weather correlation, as elsewhere.
Post-frontal sharp rises are slow days on the harbour too.
Large swell pushes surge and colour inside the Heads and can unsettle the lower harbour — a minor effect only.
LOW EVIDENCE. Solunar folklore, small weight, disclosed.

environments.name: Boat & offshore
environments.tagline: Outside the Heads — reefs, FADs and open water

factors.justification: The gatekeeper offshore. Under 10 kn is pleasant, 10–15 workable, over 18–20 is a safety and seasickness problem regardless of how good everything else looks. Weighted heaviest for that reason.
Combined with wind this decides whether the day is comfortable, fishable or cancelled. Bar crossings and anchoring on reef both degrade fast past 2 m.
Offshore is where SST matters most — EAC pulses of warm water bring kingfish, dolphinfish and the pelagic season with them.
Dawn starts are standard offshore practice — calmest sea state of the day and the best bite. Scored window ends earlier because afternoon sea breeze typically ends the day.
Opposite sign to the rocks: at sea, long-period swell is a comfortable roll while short-period wind chop is what beats a trailer boat up.
Current matters on the reefs and around the change, though less dominant than inshore.
A big settled high is exactly the forecast that makes an offshore day possible at all.
A fast fall offshore means the window is closing — plan around it.
LOW EVIDENCE. Solunar folklore, small weight, disclosed.
The definitive after-dark predator. Daytime mulloway are the exception, not the plan.
The classic Sydney mulloway trigger: a decent fresh pushing colour and bait down the system. Full floods are too much.
Run-out through deep estuary holes and bridge lines is the standard ambush setup.
The hour around the change — especially the top of the tide at night — is the storied jewie window.
Caught year-round in Sydney; estuary fishing traditionally peaks through winter.
Bigger tides push more bait through the holes.
LOW EVIDENCE, but the new/full bias is unusually persistent in mulloway lore, so it carries slightly more weight here than elsewhere — still small and disclosed.
Falling glass before a change is a repeated theme in mulloway captures.
Tolerant of cool water — part of why they headline winter.

lures: 5–7" soft plastic paddle tails worked slow and deep
rigs: Running sinker straight to a 6/0–8/0 circle or suicide hook (estuary/harbour)
Star sinker paternoster or running rig with 7/0+ for the beach
guide.leader: 40–60 lb fluorocarbon or hard mono, ~1 m
guide.rod: 9–10'6" surf rod off the sand; 7' 6–10 kg rod in the estuary
guide.season_notes: Year-round with a winter estuary peak; the days after decent rain are the days to go.
guide.habitat: Deep estuary holes and bridge pylons on the run-out after rain; beach gutters well after dark; washy headlands into the night. Think depth, current and darkness.
guide.safety_note: Night sessions: tell someone where you are, and never fish rock ledges alone in the dark.

factors.justification: The single strongest kingfish variable in Sydney: the bite switches on with warm EAC water, reliably from ~19 °C.
October–May is the Sydney season, peaking in high summer; winter fish exist but are a specialist pursuit.
First light at the markers is the appointment. They keep feeding through the day more than most inshore species.
Current past vertical structure is what lines kings up to be caught.
The change at a marker or ledge is a known trigger window.
Mostly a boat-handling limit; kings themselves don't mind chop.
General settled-weather correlation.
More range, more current at the structure.
LOW EVIDENCE. Minimal weight, disclosed.

guide.difficulty: Hard — strong, dirty fighters near structure
rigs: Live bait on a 5/0–7/0 circle, lightly weighted or under a balloon/float
guide.rod: 7' PE3–5 spin outfit; heavier from the stones
guide.season_notes: Warm months (Oct–May). Watch the water temp line on this site — over 19 °C, start planning.
guide.habitat: Channel markers, moorings, wharf pylons and harbour points on the tide change; offshore reefs and FADs by boat. Always near structure, always in current.
guide.safety_note: Lock the drag and expect to get reefed — fish heavier than feels polite.

factors.justification: Bream hold on structure and eat what the run brings past — the run-out along a rock wall or oyster lease is the classic drift.
Low light lowers their notorious caution; lit wharves fish well into the night.
A modest fresh gets bream feeding on washed-in food; a flood pushes them down the system.
Feeding tempo rises with water temperature.
Genuinely year-round in Sydney — one of the reasons they're the ideal beginner target — with a warm-month edge and a winter surf-beach run.
The species behind most 'barometer' folklore — the settled-high correlation is strongest in shallow bream water.
Sharp swings shut shallow fish down first.
More water over the leases and flats to feed on.
The last of the run-out concentrates fish at drop-offs.
Comfort and casting accuracy for light-line work.
LOW EVIDENCE. Minimal weight, disclosed.

guide.difficulty: Easy to catch, hard to master
lures: Surface poppers over flats in summer
rigs: Light running sinker (or no weight at all) to a size 2–1/0 hook — the less weight the better
guide.season_notes: All year. The most reliable 'just want to catch something' target on this list.
guide.habitat: Pontoons, rock walls, wharf pylons, oyster leases and moored boats on the run-out; surf gutters in winter. If it's structure with current, there's a bream on it.

factors.justification: The most light-gated species here: tailor sessions are dawn and dusk appointments, almost to the minute.
Autumn–winter is the Sydney beach run; summer fish are smaller and scattered.
Tailor are white-water hunters — they want turbulence and foam, not calm.
A working onshore builds the wash they hunt in.
Tide movement through the gutters moves the bait schools they follow.
A cooler-water species — part of why they own autumn and winter.
Stronger flushes through the gutters.
General settled/gentle-fall correlation.
Minor factor for a fish that likes rough edges anyway.
LOW EVIDENCE. Minimal weight, disclosed.

baits: WA pilchards on ganged hooks (the institution)
rigs: 3–4 x 4/0 ganged hooks under a star or ball sinker, rigged so the pilchard swims straight
guide.leader: 25–40 lb hard mono — their teeth destroy light leader
guide.rod: 9–10' surf rod; 7–8' spin rod off the rocks
guide.season_notes: Autumn and winter, first and last light. Set an alarm; the window is real and it is short.
guide.habitat: Beach gutters with white water at dawn/dusk; washy headlands. Follow the birds — working terns mean bait, and bait means tailor.
guide.safety_note: Fingers out of mouths — tailor bite back.

factors.justification: Schools sit in current lines and deep holes, feeding on what the tide delivers.
The cool-season counterpart to kingfish in the harbour — winter is trevally time.
A genuinely cool-water preference, opposite sign to the kingfish curve — a clean demonstration of why species get their own profiles.
Better in low light but takeable all day in deep water, especially with berley.
The change stacks the school up in the hole.
Tolerant of a little colour; a flood pushes them out to cleaner water.
Settled-weather correlation.
As elsewhere.
Comfort and berley-trail control.
More current through the holes.
LOW EVIDENCE. Minimal weight, disclosed.

baits: Bread flesh (with a bread berley trail)
rigs: Light running sinker or unweighted bait drifted down a berley trail, size 4–1 hook
guide.season_notes: The cooler months' consolation prize that fights better than it should. Winter harbour staple.
guide.habitat: Deep harbour holes, wharf shadows and current lines on the tide change; keep a berley trail going and they'll find you.

factors.justification: Squid are sight hunters — water clarity is everything, and recent rain is the best free proxy for it. The sharpest negative-rain curve on the site, deliberately opposite to mulloway.
Dawn/dusk peaks; lit wharves make night sessions productive.
Swell stirs the weed beds and kills visibility along ocean-influenced edges.
Year-round, with the clear, cool water of winter the quiet peak.
Wind chop ruins both visibility and jig control.
Gentle flow beats ripping current — squid hover over the weed rather than fight the tide.
Broad tolerance; mild preference for the middle of the range.
Settled weather = clear water, mostly an indirect effect.
As elsewhere.
Mild — enough movement to bring feed over the beds without blowing visibility.
LOW EVIDENCE. Dark nights concentrate squid under artificial lights, which nudges this slightly above the default folklore weight.

baits: Optional: baited spike jig with a whole yakka under a float
lures: Size 2.5–3.5 squid jigs; naturals in bright sun, glow/UV at dawn, dusk and night
rigs: Jig tied direct to the leader. Cast past the weed edge, let it sink, two slow whips of the rod, long pause — they take on the pause
guide.rod: Any light 7' spin rod works; a dedicated egi rod is a luxury, not a requirement
guide.season_notes: All year. Winter's clear water is quietly the best of it. Also the best fresh bait you can own — catch two, fish one, keep one for the kingfish.
guide.habitat: Weed beds and kelp-fringed edges in clear harbour water; wharf lights after dark. If you can see the bottom, you're in squid country.
guide.safety_note: Hold them head-away for the first ten seconds unless you want the ink.

factors.justification: Around all day; thicker around lights at night. The flattest curve on the site because berley matters more than any condition.
Comfort and keeping a berley trail tight.
Some flow carries the berley; too much scatters it.
Dirty water thins the schools at the wharves.
Genuinely year-round — the reason they're the live-bait currency of Sydney.
Broad tolerance.
Minor.
Minor.
Minor.
LOW EVIDENCE. Minimal weight, disclosed.

baits: Tiny cubes of pilchard, prawn or bread on the hook
lures: Sabiki bait jigs — drop, jiggle, wind up two at a time
rigs: Size 8–12 long-shank under a small float or lightly weighted; or a sabiki rig with a small sinker on the bottom
guide.rod: Anything light; a $30 combo is genuinely fine
guide.season_notes: All year, every wharf. The gateway fish — and the live bait that catches the kingfish and mulloway above.
guide.habitat: Wharf pylons and moored-boat shadows in the harbour and lower estuary. Start a bread berley trail and they appear in minutes.
guide.safety_note: Keeping them alive: an aerated bucket, change the water often, don't overcrowd it.
